data.title: Our Green Zone Buy Signal Of April 7th Is Rapidly Winding Down
data.description:
On April 7th, we notified our subscribers that our Short-Term Master Sentiment Indicator (ST-MSI) was giving a Green Zone buy signal. We’ve indicated it using a green arrow. It was the first ST-MSI buy signal since November 2023.

The indicator is now rapidly approaching the Red Zone, which would be the first sell signal since January. If it does give a sell signal it will present us with a dilemma.

The Theory of Contrary Opinion and Why it Works

At the Sentiment King we believe in the theory of contrary opinion. The theory holds that stock prices go opposite what investors expect when “too many” investors have that expectation. If “too many” investors expect prices to rise, they'll decline. If “too many” expect prices to decline, they’ll rise.

The usual explanation of why the theory works, is this: if everyone is bullish, investors have already purchased, so even with more good news, there are few buyers left to push prices higher. The advance is “exhausted.” So just a little profit taking will move prices lower.

On the other hand, if everyone is bearish, they have already sold. So, even with more bad news, there are few sellers left to push prices lower. The market is “washed out.” So, just a little buying by bargain hunters will move prices higher.

It's a theory that's been used by every great investor throughout history. To apply it, however, you need some way to know when there are “too many.” That’s what the ST-MSI is for.

The ST-MSI

Over the years, numerous indicators have been developed to know when “too many” investors are expecting something. We combine seven of them into one indicator, which we call the ST-MSI or Short-Term Master Sentiment Indicator. It designed to signal intermediate-term moves that last from three to six months.

The names of the seven indicators are listed across the top of this table. The position of the composite ST-MSI is shown in blue in the far-right column.

The Red Zone, Green Zone Ranking System

The seven indicators are combined using our Red-Zone, Green-Zone ranking system. The Red Zone indicates when investors are “too bullish.” The Green Zone indicates when there are “too many” bears.

Putting all the indicators on the same scale allows us to combine them into one master indicator in a mathematically consistent way. The yellow X shows where the indicator currently ranks, while the plus sign is where it ranked a week earlier.

But our red zone green zone scale also helps us with something else.

Sentiment indicators only have meaning when they show extreme levels of investor expectations. Even though an indicator might show 70% of investors bullish, that might not be enough. The indicator might only have meaning when 90% are bullish. Our Red Green Zone scale tells us this.

Red and Green Zone readings tell us when historically that indicator is at an extreme. In between the red and Green Zone is a large gray area we call the neutral zone, where the indicator is not saying anything.

Contradictions

If the ST-MSI does give a Red Zone sell signal it will present us with a slight problem.

We wrote an article on May 13th (This advance should continue through the summer and move up in two waves) strongly suggesting this rally will continue through July and August. It was based on the long held Elliott wave principle that recovery rallies after a sharp selloff occur in two waves and that the market is still in the first wave. If we get an ST-MSI sell signal, it will contradict this. Which fact will prevail?

That remains to be seen. We update the ST-MSI once a week on Saturday for our subscribers. We don’t have a buy signal yet. These charts are for last week.

If we do get a sell signal, we’ll first discuss this contradiction with our subscribers and work out what, we think, will happen. At this time, we’re not sure.

data.title: XPeng Could Surge On Record Growth, AI Breakthroughs, And Wall Street's Blind Spot
data.description:
With how things are moving in the Electric Vehicle ("EV") market, I can’t help but spot a few investment-worthy candidates within the sector. Lucid Motors (LCID) and Rivian (RIVN) are some of the EV companies you can bet on for the long-haul, thanks mainly to the powerful entities backing them: Rivian has Amazon (AMZN), while Lucid has Saudi Arabia.

However, one of their peers, XPeng Inc. (XPEV) (OTCPK:XPNGF), stood out without such powerful backers - I mean, it technically has Volkswagen (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY) Groups backing it, but it isn’t the main selling point - at least not for me. Although XPeng is still not generating a profit, the stock is up 92% YTD.

Heads up: I’m rating XPEV stock a “Buy” - and not specifically because of its recent bull run, but because of how excited I am about this company's prospects once it turns a profit.

Company Overview

Guangzhou Xiaopeng Automotive Technology is a China-based car company that sets itself apart by producing “more advanced” electric vehicles, or Smart EVs. The company was founded in 2014 and went public in 2020 through an IPO on the New York Stock Exchange.

Initially, XPeng’s figurative “bread and butter” was the G3 and P7, which are crossover SUVs and 4-door sport sedans. However, XPeng stopped the production of its G3 platform in 2023.

Today, XPeng’s product lineup includes:

  • X9 (Flagship) - Electric MPVs

  • G9 - Luxury Electric SUVs

  • G6 - Mid-Size Electric SUVs

  • P7 - Electric Sedans.

These EVs are sold in 223 cities in roughly 700 physical stores worldwide. XPeng’s primary EVs are priced between ~$24,400 and ~$57,800. Today, the company’s primary market is Europe, Asia, and Latin America, while targeting the mid to high-end segment in China’s vehicle market.

If you’re wondering about the United States, no XPeng EV is sold here, at least not right now. So, if you did happen to spot one of these on the road, they're most likely imported from other countries.

Top-Line, Bottom-Line, and Earnings-Per-Share

On May 21, 2025, XPeng reported its Q1 ’25 financials, and the figures are compelling.

The company’s top line increased by over 140% year-over-year to around $2.2 billion. Although its operating earnings came out negative at $143.5 million, the loss improved by ~37% compared to the same period last year.

XPeng’s bottom line figures are similar: a net loss of $95 million for the quarter - ~51% lower than the previous year. Naturally, this improvement is reflected in XPeng's EPS for the quarter: ($0.05) from ($0.10). The annual figures paint a similar picture. XPeng’s top line for 2024 increased by nearly 30% year-over-year, operating loss declined by almost 40%, and net loss decreased by over 46% from 2023.

The gaps are far more notable when comparing FY’20 to FY’24 figures. Its top line increased by a whopping 525% to nearly $5.6 billion. However, its net loss nearly doubled over the same period.

Digging Deeper on XPeng’s Financials

While I have an idea, I can’t help but wonder: why is XPeng’s revenue growth so stellar, yet the rest of the crucial metrics aren’t as great?

It turns out XPeng's financial setbacks stem from higher costs of revenues, primarily driven by the extensive R&D and General Expenses, which is exactly my speculation on the company.

XPeng developed an in-house Operating System for its smart EVs and its own self-driving architecture, and they even collaborated with Volkswagen to develop superchargers. XPeng is definitely not sleeping on research and development. If XPeng were volume down on R&D expenses, we’d see better overall financials, but that can’t be the case right now. The company must sacrifice near-term profitability for long-term potential.

Just like its closest industry peers, Lucid and Rivian, XPeng is still in the early development stages. For investors, it means we really can’t expect much in terms of profitability - at least not right now.

For a better comparison, I’ve included 5-yr net income data from XPeng’s closest peers:

In my most recent analysis for Lucid, I pointed out the same occurrence of EV companies being unprofitable during their "early-stage struggles." And, as you can see from the peer group, pretty much of the group is operating in red, mainly due to R&D expenses.

So that explains the contrast between XPeng’s revenue growth vs other key metrics. But, looking closely, XPeng has the narrowest gap between net loss and profitability among its peers.

Sure, some in the peer group are now profitable, but if you look closely enough, it took them a long time to get there! Tesla, for example, turned a profit for the first time in 2020.

Then there’s Li Auto, who turned to profit following revenue increasing by 1,266% between 2020 and 2024 - the highest increase by % in the peer group.

XPeng Record Delivery Volumes

On the topic of XPeng’s revenue growth, it’s driven by an increase in vehicle sales. You might think I’m only stating the obvious, but follow me.

XPeng’s vehicle sales for Q1 ‘25 increased by nearly 160% year-over-year to nearly $2 billion. Total product deliveries increased by almost 331% to 94,008 units over the same period! By far, that’s the highest number of quarterly deliveries for XPeng. The momentum continued into April of this year with over 35,000 deliveries, which brought its YTD total deliveries close to 130,000 vehicles.

For perspective, Lucid Motors delivered 3,109 vehicles in Q1’25, up 58% year-over-year, while Rivian delivered 13,588 units with around 57% growth rate in the same quarter. I mean, I’m aware that the price range for XPeng EVs are notably lower than Lucid's or Rivian’s, so it shouldn’t be a surprise for the company to have more deliveries, right? …but I can’t help it, the gap is just insane.

With how product deliveries are going, I’m confident that XPeng will hit its Q2 ‘25 delivery target of 102,000 - 108,000 vehicles, which is a more or less 250% increase year-over-year. If that momentum continues, the company should be profitable in about a year.

XPEV Stock Performance

A few months after the IPO, XPEV hit its all-time high of $64.20 per share. From there, the stock saw wild swings on the backs of early investors trying to profit from the rally and then went on a notable decline in late 2022. Since then, volatility has come down.

Right now, the stock is down by over 65% from its all-time highs. Needless to say, I can feel your pain if you’re still holding a position that you bought 5 years back. That said, the same trend can be seen from XPeng’s closest peers as well. Based on the net income comparison I presented earlier, you’d understand why this huge decline isn’t entirely unique to XPeng.

Look at Rivian’s stock performance around the same period:

RIVN stock went from an all-time high of around $130 to $16.34 - that’s a near 88% decline!

On the other hand, Lucid Motors isn't very different. LCID declined from all-time highs of around $56 to $2.77, which means a nearly 95% decline.

Even more established companies within its peer groups experienced the same downwards trend, with Tesla being an exception - that’s primarily because of how Tesla investors perceive CEO Elon Musk.

Going back to XPeng. Although XPEV price movements are primarily driven by macroeconomic forces - its 52-week price movement offers a more compelling view compared to its peers:

More importantly, XPEV stock is up by 92% Year-to-Date, while Lucid Motors and Tesla are down by around 9% and 17%, respectively. Considering that most tech and EV companies retracted in the same period, this is just impressive.

Wall Street analysts also echoed bullish sentiments for XPeng. Although I don’t really dive into these experts’ ratings, XPEV just happens to be one of the Wall Street darlings right now - consensus among 29 analysts rate the stock as a “Buy.”

Potential Catalysts for XPeng

There’s lots of positive things to talk about when it comes to XPeng, and some of these are the reasons behind my ‘buy rating’ - despite the bottlenecks.

The company’s most immediate catalyst is, arguably, the newly launched 2025 XPeng X9 - a flagship model with Turing AI Smart Driving as a standard feature. That’s a leap for the company’s autonomous driving capabilities. XPeng management highlighted the ‘accelerated adoption of physical AI technologies’ as a potential growth driver. Although it remains to be seen, the company’s emphasis on AI integration with the XPeng vehicle lineup means there’s a substantial investment to improve autonomous driving functions.

Also, CEO Xiaopeng He emphasized launching "more blockbuster products," which potentially means there will be more product introductions in the following years. I mean, we know how good XPeng is when it comes to selling their products - it even outpaced NIO in terms of vehicle deliveries.

XPeng’s partnership with Volkswagen also presents potential opportunities. In fact, the reported revenues outside vehicle sales - or ‘other services’ are said to be attributable to the platform and technical collaboration between XPeng and Volkswagen Group. Revenue from these segments contributes to the overall profitability while the company scales up vehicle production. Although I don’t know how long the partnership will last, XPeng got its hands on Volkswagen’s technologies, which are highly competitive in the EV business.

Tesla’s eroding market share also presents an opportunity for companies like XPeng, though certainly in the United States anytime soon. I’ve always been vocal about my take on Tesla, and how Elon Musk’s direct involvement with politics is affecting the company.

Personally, I know Tesla owners who sold their EVs because they don’t want to be associated with what’s happening with the controversies surrounding the CEO. It’s a more critical decision considering that protesters from different parts of the world are targeting Tesla properties and showrooms.

In short, Tesla’s reputation is definitely not in good shape right now, which is why deliveries and production are declining.

More importantly, despite Tesla’s setbacks, there’s been a surge in EV sales, especially in China - where XPeng and most of its closest peers originate. This is why I think Tesla’s not-so-good situation benefits other EVs, which are often more affordable, have a better price-to-value ratio, aesthetics, range, and, most importantly, no resentment from Tesla protesters. You can sleep well at night knowing your car won’t be targeted by some angry mob at the stop light.

My Verdict

Companies like XPeng aren't exactly the type of company that I'm known to bet the farm on. As an income investor, I’d naturally be comfortable investing in companies with time-tested business models and stable dividend income.

That said, diversification is something that investors must do, and XPEV is definitely a stock worth-considering. The company is ramping up production as a result of incredibly high demand. And although it’s still operating at a loss, it’s closest to profitability compared to its peers.

If XPeng’s momentum continues and targets are realized - which is quite likely to happen with how things are moving - I think investors will be very sorry if they missed out on buying a position at these prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. House Passes 'One Big Beautiful Bill' Act: Sets The Stage For A Massive Fiscal Impulse
data.description:
Early this morning, the U.S. House of Representatives passed a broad spending bill that includes adjustments to government outlays and President Trump’s tax cuts. The bill has been long debated and eventually passed by a one vote margin to move to the US Senate for debate and voting there. As passed, the legislation package would be a massive loosening of fiscal policy and add trillions to the deficit over the next decade.

Thin Margins

At about 7:00 am EST this morning, the House of Representatives concluded a long night of debate with a successful vote to pass H.R. 1, also known as the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act,” by a very thin margin of 215 to 214. The votes were split across party lines, as all Democrats voted against the bill, and all but three Republicans voted for it. Two of those three Republicans were nays, while one simply voted “present,” a decision to abstain from voting. The piece of legislation had been adjusted over the last two days to ensure that the Republicans could finally pass the bill with their thin majority in the House.

Spending Bill Details

The enormous spending bill is over 1,000 pages long and includes many things that will impact federal receipts and outlays. The largest parts of the legislation include various tax reforms that, on net, are set to increase the federal deficit:

  • The headlining action of the bill is that it would make the tax specifications under Trump’s 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which are set to expire at the end of this year, permanent. This means individual income tax rate cuts and a nearly doubled standard deduction from the 2017 law would not sunset. The standard deduction is further increased, for example, the standard deduction for joint filers would rise to $32,000 starting in 2026.
  • Alongside making the TCJA rates permanent, the bill locks in the doubled child tax credit from TCJA and even boosts it slightly (to $2,500 per child, up from the current $2,000). It also maintains the lower estate tax (the higher exemption from 2017) and other individual provisions, ensuring the Trump-era tax regime remains in place beyond 2025. Other details include tax breaks for “pass-through” businesses, multinational corporations, and extended business tax breaks from 2017.
  • In addition to extending prior cuts, H.R. 1 introduces new tax breaks that President Trump campaigned on in 2024. Notably, the bill eliminates federal taxes on tipping, certain overtime pay, and automobile loan interest for individual taxpayers. These new measures are set to expire in 2029. The legislation also creates an “enhanced deduction” for seniors: retirees of certain income levels would get an extra $4,000 deduction to reduce taxes on Social Security.
  • One of the most contentious tax issues was the cap on SALT deductions. The 2017 TCJA capped SALT deductions at $10,000, which disproportionately affected high-tax states. If TCJA expired, that cap would disappear in 2026, restoring full deductibility. House Republicans chose to raise the SALT deduction cap rather than eliminate it, as a compromise. Initially, H.R. 1 tripled the cap to $30,000 for joint filers (with income limits), but the final bill was amended to quadruple the SALT cap to roughly $40,000 (for taxpayers earning under $500,000 per year).

With tax reform set to increase the deficit, the bill includes other provisions that look to offset that effect by raising revenue or cutting spending. Ultimately, these measures would not do enough to make the bill budget-neutral:

  • The bill includes various measures that will add new revenues or create savings. Tax breaks for electric vehicles and other green energy will end. Health benefits for immigrants are set to be reduced, and eligibility requirements for the Affordable Care Act ("ACA") would be stricter. In terms of raising taxes, H.R. 1 would increase the tax rate on the largest private university endowments and add a 5% tax on funds sent by immigrants to their home countries.
  • The bill also includes direct or indirect spending cuts to try and offset the decline in income. H.R. 1 includes many changes to Medicaid that would bring enrollment down by an estimated 7.7 million people (a more than 10% drop). Funding to support green energy projects in the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act and other environmental initiatives is set to be cancelled. Like the changes to Medicaid, eligibility for SNAP (food stamps) would be tightened, which would likely reduce participation. Finally, the legislation updated terms to federal student loan programs, which would likely hinder ongoing loan forgiveness initiatives.
  • As if massive tax cuts weren’t deficit spending enough, the bill includes some new spending that offsets some of the cuts. Military outlays are set to increase with plans for increases in funding for shipbuilding, air & missile defense (the “Golden Dome”), nuclear weapons, and border security. In addition to military spending increases on border security, the bill adds funding to Homeland Security for the border wall construction, staffing, and surveillance.

Deficit Impact

The CBO was unable to give a complete cost estimate for the final bill that was passed, but it has analyzed most parts of the bill and how they would impact the deficit. The most current estimation suggests that the tax reform would increase the federal deficit by $3.8 trillion over the next ten years, while the spending cuts and meager new revenues would save around $1.5 trillion. Another estimate by the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget [CRFB], which is a little less current, signals it will add $3.3 trillion through FY2034, and potentially more if the temporary tax cuts are extended. It also estimates that interest costs would rise to $1.8 to 1.9 trillion and total debt to 125% to 129% of GDP.

The impact on the deficit is not flat over the next decade. Most of the pressure on the deficit will occur starting in FY26, when the new tax rules are put in place. The CBO estimates that from FY26 to FY29, the deficit will be higher by at least $350 billion in each of those years, with the largest increase in FY27 of just under $560 billion. The impact only becomes smaller after that five-year period because several temporary tax measures will expire, but that doesn’t mean they won’t be extended. Additionally, it will take some time for the spending cuts to have a major impact on the deficit. According to the CBO, the non-tax related measures will not reduce the deficit by $200 billion or more until FY31.

In the end, the budget bill H.R. 1, if passed, will result in a strong expansionary fiscal impulse driven by tax cuts to high-income individuals, and based on the structure of the costs, most of that impact will be seen in the next four years (during the Trump presidency). To many who thought the Trump administration would make moves that tighten fiscal policy, the passage of this bill is tone-deaf to debt sustainability concerns, especially since it comes less than a week after Moody’s downgraded the US long-term debt rating from Aaa to Aa1. These events have caused long-term US Treasury rates to continue their upward trajectories, as the 20-year and 30-year yields both move above 5%. In fact, the news today has pushed the 30-year Treasury yield up around 2 bps to 5.11%, the highest since 2007.

The legislation has now moved to the Senate, where there also exists a slim Republican majority. If it passes as it exists now, there is no reason to believe that deficit spending won’t increase substantially over the next four years. The initial economic impact might be strong as consumption and investment will likely be bolstered by lower taxes for consumers and businesses, but there will be a significant response in financial markets that increases long-term borrowing costs and stymies growth in equities through higher discount rates.

In addition to the response from financial markets, the Federal Reserve would have to respond to a loosening in fiscal policy with a shift to a more restrictive baseline monetary policy that could delay any further rate cuts in 2025. The tax reform in particular would put upward pressure on inflation expectations (that are already high from tariffs) and threaten the FOMC’s inflation stability mandate. Thus, we would probably see short-term interest rates eventually move up and the entire yield curve would rise.

With the spending bill being so broad, there are so many different dynamics to consider for investors when trying to navigate the potential impacts on equities. There is one main theme that should be the focus: H.R. 1 presents serious debt sustainability concerns that would create more problems than it solves. The more we push the problem down the road, the bigger the consequences will (eventually) be.

data.title: Zai Lab Limited: Banking On Chinese Pharma Growth
data.description:
Top Line Summary and Update

Zai Lab Limited (NASDAQ:ZLAB) is a company I've covered a few times, most recently last December, when I felt that the company had turned a corner toward an optimistic outlook.

Pipeline Updates

In my last article, I highlighted the various products that generate revenue for ZLAB, notably niraparib (Zejula), efgartigimod alfa (Yvygrat), omadacycline (Nuzyra), and Optune, all licensed from companies outside of China.

ZLAB also has in its hands various licenses for compounds likely to be approved shortly in China. These include the RET inhibitor repotrectinib (accepted for approval review back in April), the TF antibody-drug conjugate tisotumab vedotin (accepted for review with Chinese regulators in March and approved in the United States back in late April), and potential expansion of the Optune label into pancreatic cancer, which will be featured in a late-breaking abstract at ASCO.

Other important in-licensed drugs include ripretinib and bemarituzumab, just to give you a sense of the array of partnerships the company has built to leverage already-developed programs.

All of these are strong developments for the company's licensing arm, and it signals ongoing expansion of revenues throughout 2025 and 2026. But today, I also want to take a look at some of ZLAB's in-house developments as well. Currently, there are 2 clinical programs in the ZLAB oncology pipeline.

ZL-1310

This project is ZLAB's entry into the space of targeting DLL3, the Notch ligand that is almost ubiquitously expressed in most neuroendocrine neoplasms, which include your GEP-NETs, large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma, and small cell lung cancer.

The latter tumor area has gained by far the most attention for DLL3 as a target, with the approval of the bispecific T cell engager tarlatamab last year. The last year has seen enormously important advances in the management of small cell lung cancer, and ASCO 2025 is going to feature some critical updates in the disease space (specifically the IMforte trial and DELLphi-304, both positive studies).

ZL-1310 is an antibody-drug conjugate targeting DLL3, and it's being explored in a phase 1 trial in extensive-stage SCLC. ZLAB announced that they will be presenting results from this study in a poster presentation at ASCO. The most recent catalyst for the company (at the time of writing) was the announcement that the US FDA has granted Fast Track designation to ZL-1310.

This opens the door to potential accelerated approval, which the company thinks could come as early as 2027.

ZL-1218

ZLAB is also working on an antibody targeted against CCR8, a molecule understood to help the tumor suppress T cells. It is in phase 1 study in patients with solid tumors, and we got a preliminary data readout last year demonstrating evidence that ZL-1218 could modulate the tumor microenvironment.

Financial Overview

As of their most recent quarterly earnings, ZLAB had cash and equivalents of $757.3 million. They had $173.4 million in short-term debt. Product revenues were $105.7 million, up from $87.1 million in Q4 2024. After cost of revenue and operating expenses, the loss from operations reached $56.3 million, and the net loss after interest was $48.4 million for the quarter.

Net cash used for operations was $61.7 million. Extrapolating this to an annual negative cash flow of $246.8 million, ZLAB has the cash on hand to fund operations for approximately 3 years.

Strengths and Risks

Strength - A diverse, thriving pipeline mixed with approved and developmental agents

ZLAB's partnerships and in-house developmental programs provide a lot of stability in addition to carving out its own path. By leveraging already-built programs, the company has a revenue stream that supports development, and these revenues are growing at a healthy clip. Of course, in-licensing has costs, with a gross profit margin of only around 64%. So ZLAB has a strongly vested interest in getting their own programs off the ground.

Risk - Risky pipeline projects

But let's talk about their in-house development. Specifically, ZL-1312, the DLL3 antibody-drug conjugate, is heir to an ignominious legacy in the space of small cell lung cancer.

At this time, it is undeniable that DLL3 is a critical target in neuroendocrine carcinomas. Tarlatamab has blown the door wide open. But before tarlatamab came Rova-T, an antibody-drug conjugate targeting DLL3. I'm sure that sounds familiar?

Those who have been around for a moment might remember that Rova-T was looking very promising for quite a while, but it ended up failing due mainly to issues related to toxicity and subpar evidence of activity. It was one of the high-profile drug failures of the 2010s in the oncology space.

ZL-1312 has a lot of proving to do if ZLAB is going to make a good case for its inclusion in the standard of care for small cell, which has been a drug graveyard until just the last decade, and most of the gains have come in the form of immunotherapy.

Strength - Cash sufficient to continue aggressive expansion of their own agents

ZLAB maintains a healthy balance sheet, with no clear need for a cash raise in the near future. The recent liquidation of their short-term investments further bolstered their cash position, and at this time their liquidity remains an unequivocal strength, in my view.

Bottom Line Summary

ZLAB presents an interesting, two-pronged attack on the challenges of drug development and generating some kind of value proposition. By focusing on high-quality partnerships enabling revenue generation based on Chinese product sales, they have a healthy revenue stream, even if they're still pretty far off from positive earnings.

Meanwhile, they're able to tackle their own in-house projects, possibly giving them a substantial boost toward profitability if successful.

When I tell you that ZLAB's in-house efforts make them a phase 1 developmental-stage biotech, you should chafe at the valuation, which stands at over $3.5 billion at the time of writing. But the in-licensing attack that's generating growing revenues definitely provides important color to the story and dramatically improves the investment thesis.

This valuation is relatively generous, of course, with an EV-to-sales ratio of 7.1, compared with a sector median of 3.16. But the forward EV to sales is a more modest 5.25 (forward P/S is 6.25). These data suggest that the market is optimistic about the company's growth prospects, and I'm inclined to be, too, despite the risks that are present for the company's internal pipeline.

At this time, I maintain my relative optimism and "Buy" sentiment on ZLAB. I think this is very much worthy of your attention.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Focused Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

  • Entering 2025, markets had benefited from a prolonged macroeconomic period that was supportive of equities, and especially for growth stocks.
  • Economic data signaled a healthy market climate, the Federal Reserve began a series of rate cuts as inflation had moderated, and there was some optimism around the potential effect of the second Trump administration's economic policies.
  • Uncertainty, however, was also rising. While it was expected that President Trump's agenda would shape the broader investment landscape this year, greater uncertainty around implementation of his policies-trade and tariffs, tax reform, immigration, etc.-created new risks.
  • As new tariffs were announced during the first quarter of 2025, it was unclear as to the magnitude of the import levies and their use as a negotiating tool.
  • Volatility rose given heightened concerns that increased input costs and supply chain disruptions would lead to higher prices and dampen consumer spending. Sentiment began to wane amidst a rising tide of uncertainty.
  • Both the S&P 500 and Russell 1000 Growth indices posted their first negative quarter in almost two years.
  • For the quarter, the PGIM Jennison Focused Growth Fund (MUTF:SPFZX) declined −11.7%, which was behind the Russell 1000 Growth Index, which fell −10.0%.

SECTOR PERFORMANCE

  • The Russell 1000 Growth Index fell −10.0%% in the first quarter of 2025.
  • Communication Services, Utilities, Information Technology, and Consumer Discretionary were the worst performing sectors.
  • Energy, Real Estate, and Health Care were the best performing sectors.

FUND PERFORMANCE

  • The PGIM Jennison Focused Growth Fund fell -11.7%, underperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index, which fell 10.0%.

    • Shares of UBER surged after an activist investor took a stake in the company.
    • MercadoLibre (MELI) shares surged as strong earnings alleviated concerns from the previous quarter's results.
    • Netflix (NFLX) continues to outdistance its competitors in owned and licensed content, driving growth in subscriptions domestically as well as in underpenetrated non-U.S. markets. Price flexibility plus expanded ad tiers remain opportunities and should benefit free cash flow margins.

    • Shares of Nvidia (NVDA) lost value amid the momentum unwind and a broader selloff in AI-related stocks.
    • Broadcom (AVGO) declined amid the momentum unwind and a broader selloff in AI-related stocks.
    • Amazon (AMZN) shares lost value due to concerns over the company's increase in capital expenditures.
    • After shares rose dramatically in the fourth quarter, Tesla (TSLA) lost value in the first quarter due to controversies with the company's CEO and a tougher sales environment.

  • As we look forward into 2025, challenges continue to intensify. On April 2nd, President Trump initiated a series of tariffs impacting countries exporting to the United States, building upon existing trade measures with nations such as Canada and Mexico. These new tariffs raise the overall weighted average to approximately 25%, a level unseen in nearly a century. This policy shift, more aggressive than anticipated, elicited a negative market reaction, with equity prices losing significant value following the announcement, signaling concerns about U.S. economic growth, and rising inflationary pressures.
  • The staggered deadlines for implementation of tariffs, which involve a phased rollout of duties on specific goods and trading partners, provide an opportunity for countries to negotiate; however, should negotiations fail, retaliatory measures, as demonstrated by China, are increasingly likely. The existing trade relationship with Mexico, now further strained, introduces additional vulnerabilities given the interconnectedness of supply chains.
  • Immediate concerns center on the potential impact on overall consumer demand, inflation, input costs, margins, and profits. The magnitude of this policy change requires careful monitoring of how our government's actions will influence trading partners, as retaliatory measures could pose a significant headwind to economic expansion while simultaneously fueling inflation.
  • The administration's aggressive and antagonistic approach to global trade is without precedent in our lifetimes. While acknowledging that the landscape is rapidly evolving, we are focused on navigating its complexities. We anticipate that economic growth will slow, with inflation likely to rise. Companies will likely face mounting pressure on profit margins, depressing earnings and valuation assumptions. Against this evolving backdrop, we are integrating these dynamics into the Fund's construction activity. We remain focused on identifying resilient opportunities in the face of the challenging trade landscape and resulting and fluid market dynamics.

data.description:
In the near-term, the new administration's long-term pro-growth policies will likely come with short-term "disruption."

Market Review

Equity markets were mixed during the first quarter, as US stocks retreated amid a landscape of tariff threats, inflation fears and declining consumer confidence. Meanwhile, international and emerging-market stocks moved higher but pared gains in March after trade-war concerns spread. For the quarter, the S&P 500® Index fell -4.27%, while the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of non-US developed markets, gained 6.86% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index advanced 2.93%. Tariff uncertainty and extended valuations caused a pullback in technology stocks, leading growth stocks to underperform their value peers. Heightened economic uncertainty sent small-cap stocks markedly lower; large-cap stocks also retreated into negative territory.

Quarterly Performance

BNY Mellon Dynamic Value Fund (Class A at NAV) returned 2.63% during the first quarter of 2025. In comparison, the fund’s benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index (the “Index”), returned 2.14% for the same time period.

Market Review (continued)

In the US, equities fell as sector performance reversed course. While the energy and health-care sectors gained for the first quarter, the sectors that had benefited from artificial-intelligence enthusiasm in 2024 -- namely, consumer discretionary, information technology and communication services -- dragged the S&P 500 lower. Consumer confidence plunged on increasing concerns over the impact of tariffs on the nation’s economy. The Federal Reserve left interest rates unchanged at both of its meetings in the first quarter, with inflation remaining stubbornly above the central bank’s 2% target. As President Trump’s tariff threats grew over the quarter, consumer spending weakened and flash manufacturing surveys showed a sharp slowing in business growth. The US dollar was volatile, and the 10-year Treasury yield ended the quarter lower.

Developed international markets advanced, although tariff woes cut into quarterly gains after equities fell in March. Easing inflation and slowing growth in the eurozone prompted the European Central Bank to cut rates twice, while tariff threats had the central bank downgrade its 2025 economic growth forecast. Germany, the region’s largest economy, experienced negative economic growth in the final quarter of 2024 and announced a multi-billion-euro fiscal package to boost the country’s economy. During the first quarter, the Bank of England lowered rates by 25 basis points and cut its growth forecast amid stubborn inflation and weak growth. Japanese stocks posted significant declines amid tariff uncertainty, particularly as the US is Japan’s second-largest trading partner. Even as core inflation increased, the Bank of Japan held rates steady, announcing that it would wait to see the full impact of global trade tariffs before making any policy adjustments.

Emerging-market equities experienced modest gains as the US dollar slumped and Chinese equities sharply increased. While the People’s Bank of China (PBOC) left the benchmark lending rate unchanged during the first quarter, the central bank phased out the medium-term lending facility rate, allowing shorter-term instruments to play a larger role in steering markets. The PBOC also conducted reverse repurchase agreements to inject liquidity into markets. Brazilian markets reported solid gains amid low valuations and high foreign inflows. However, major exporters, such as Taiwan, dropped over the quarter. India also declined amid foreign selling and concerns over the health of the US and Indian economies.

Commodities moved higher during the quarter as investors sought the safety of gold, sending the yellow metal to new highs. Copper prices also surged, fueled by tariff threats on the in-demand metal used in electronic devices and components. While natural-gas prices increased, oil prices moved lower on weak US consumer confidence and economic data. For the quarter, the Dow Jones Commodity Index surged 7.20%.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000 Value Index, during the first quarter of 2025. Stock selection in the information technology and financials sectors contributed, while stock selection in the industrials and consumer discretionary sectors detracted.

SECTOR REVIEW 1

Positive Impacts

Information Technology: Stock selection was beneficial in the information technology sector, highlighted by not owning several underperforming names in the semiconductor and semiconductor equipment segment.

Financials: Stock selection contributed to returns particularly within capital markets and financial services.

Negative Impacts

Industrials: Stock selection in the industrials sector lagged, particularly in the electrical equipment subsector as Hubbell Incorporated (HUBB) was a key detractor over the period.

Consumer Discretionary: Stock selection within the sector weighed on performance, in particular stock selection within the hotels, restaurants and leisure subsector.

STOCK REVIEW 1

Positive Impacts

AT&T: Shares of the telecommunications company performed solidly over the period as the company reiterated its full year earnings guidance and commitment to hitting financial targets for the full year.

Newmont Corporation (NEM): Shares of the gold producer Newmont were solid over the period as gold prices appreciated past $3,000 per ounce to reach a new all-time high.

EQT Corporation (EQT): Shares of natural gas producer EQT were strong over the period as colder temperatures and increased demand lifted natural gas prices.

Negative Impacts

Hubbell: Shares of the electronic components and equipment maker were weak over the period after the company reported earnings that surpassed consensus expectations but fell short on sales. The company also offered a more conservative outlook for the full year.

Las Vegas Sands Corp. (LVS): Shares of leisure and gaming company Las Vegas Sands were weak for the quarter following results that missed investors’ expectations. Some of the company’s key properties in Asia have been slow to re-open following a renovation period. The company also announced a management change late in the period.

Danaher Corporation: Shares of life science and device company Danaher finished the quarter lower after reporting earnings that missed investor expectations and the company’s previous guidance.

1 Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

Market Outlook

Back in December, we published our 2025 outlook and discussed navigating “known unknowns.” The first “known unknown” we highlighted was how the rhetoric and promises from the campaign trail would translate into reality. Tariffs were one of the big campaign trail topics, and now, after a few months of starts and stops, they have become a reality. The size and scale of the announced tariffs caught the markets off-guard and ratcheted up uncertainty around economic growth, inflation, corporate earnings and potential changes in monetary policy. In early April, the announced tariffs also led to the largest two-day equity market sell-off since the early days of the Covid-19 pandemic.

Despite the near-term uncertainty, we remain optimistic about the US markets. If the new administration follows through on its pro-growth policies, such as tax extensions/tax cuts and deregulation, like it did on tariffs, we think it could support more sustainable economic and corporate earnings growth. The next few weeks should provide a lot of good information as companies start to report their first-quarter earnings and provide their outlook for the rest of the year. As usual, we will consider the macro factors through the lens of the companies we are looking to hold in our strategy.

As we mentioned in our outlook last month, we believe this could be the start of a longer-term, more sustainable period for value leadership in the US equity markets. In the near-term, the new administration's long-term pro-growth policies will likely come with short-term "disruption." These types of periods typically favor companies with stronger fundamentals, current earnings and cash flow growth, and less demanding valuations, and are typically found in the more value-oriented areas of the market. Over the medium- to longer-term, the new administration's pro-growth policies will likely stimulate value-oriented areas of the market, like energy and financials. Furthermore, artificial intelligence will be more of a benefit to Wall Street and Main Street over Silicon Valley and earnings growth expectations should start to normalize, including among the Magnificent Seven names.

We continue to believe experienced active managers with long-tenured teams that employ a consistent and repeatable process are best suited in this type of environment and continue to think that investing in companies that sit at the nexus of strong and improving fundamentals, attractive valuation and catalyst-driven business momentum have the ability to deliver the best investment outcomes for our clients.
